vulnerabilities.description: msg is passed directly as the format string argument to printf; if msg contains format specifiers (e.g. %n, %x), an attacker who controls the message can read or corrupt memory.
gets() is an inherently dangerous function with no bounds checking; it writes as many bytes as the user supplies into buf regardless of its size.
Because gets() performs no bounds checking, input longer than the caller-supplied buf causes a stack-based buffer overflow, overwriting adjacent stack frames and the return address.
strcpy(tmp, line) has no size check; if line is LINE_SIZE (256) bytes or longer, it overflows the fixed-size tmp[LINE_SIZE] stack buffer.
log_error(line) passes the raw line from the config file as the format string to printf inside log_error; a config file line containing format specifiers enables format string exploitation.
log_error(path) passes the user-supplied file path as the format string; a crafted path containing format specifiers (e.g. %n) will be interpreted by printf inside log_error.
malloc can return NULL; entries is used on the very next line (*count = 0 writes through entries) without a NULL check, causing a NULL pointer dereference on allocation failure.
entries[i].value is user-controlled data read from a config file and passed directly to system(); an attacker who can write the config file can execute arbitrary OS commands.
